item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Over 4 years since last comment, I wonder what causes great articles like this to fall into the forgotten zone…

Hopefully my comment brings some more views and well deserved upvotes. I added my +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always saw notes like that as something the Foundation would want in reports. The Foundation deals with many objects that have an effect on perception (such as this one), and having notes from the researcher would help later readers see exactly how he/she perceived the object. Just my two cents in a necropost.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd pass this one to the philosophers, if we had any

Golden, just golden. This is the thing upvotes are made from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is charming if kinda bare-bones. I feel like we need more description of this guy in "human" form. I guess by the lack of info he must be pretty boring, but it's weird to me that even a physical description is lacking.

Also, when did this "condition" start? Cameras are EVERY WHERE in modern society. Is their security CCTV footage of a speeding camera milling around in the interior of a 7-11 somewhere?

Neutral vote, but I'm fond of this one. Just the right level of weird for me personally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The doctor's notes killed it for me. I don't need some guy shoving in my face that this might be a camera masquerading as a man; I want to question that by myself.
No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whimsical and weird as all hell. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How 'bout trying to hook it/him up with a nice 40-45 year old D-Class? Would it even be interested in having a relationship, and if so, would it be fruitful?

(…mental image of Geena Davis in "The Fly" giving birth, but instead of a giant maggot out pops a Go-Pro or some junk…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Notice for the copyright of the image:

Released into the public domain by the copyright holder.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Truvelo_speed_camera.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview that wasn't there felt conspicuous by it's absence. If you didn't feel that way and it did work for you, then by all means, upvote.

I don't really know how to respond to the idea that my vote was unfair because my reaction to the article wasn't what it was supposed to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very weird. I liked the doctor's notes on the tests, especially the last one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: :D I'm glad you enjoyed that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Notes: I was driving on private land! I'm not paying it. - Doctor Littlefield.

+1+1+1+1+1+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exactly. :)

There are no doubt many more tests that explored the other senses, but they wouldn't be included in the containment document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Easy enough to address that and maintain the core:
All direct observations, not just vision, by a human indicate he's human.
All indirect observations or via instrumentation indicate camera.

It boils down to what do you trust more, your own eyes or the objective instruments? Because one of the two is lying to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You realize that a lot of articles about sapient SCPs don't have interview logs in them, right? If you're using their presence as a standard for judging those articles, it's completely unsupported by any aspects of the articles you've read.

And it is plausible, because the Foundation would not find it relevant to include an interview if it didn't include any actionable or explanatory information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It really is an honour to have created something that gets into people's heads. Thank you for reading!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your down vote is extremely unfair, at least no vote it if your only reason is that it is lacking an interview log. He could include a 200 page novel of interviews, but obviously nobody cares for that. Would it make the SCP that much more interesting to include interviews of a regular person speaking? Most definitely not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was just thinking about this SCP while doing work. I really like it if I didn't mention that before

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of course they would interview him, and perform many, many more tests.

But if those interviews and tests don't add anything useful to the *containment* of the SCP, it wouldn't be included in this document. I don't think any of the documents on here represent a full showing of every bit of information held about the SCPs.

So I think your assessment is unfair. But it's yours to make.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting idea, writing is ok but there's no way they wouldn't interview him, at least in the course of testing.

Not plausible as a Foundation document due to that lack, so -1 from me, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had never imagined being a researcher until this article.
I just imagined watching this dude do some seemingly normal mundane task, then checking the video feed later that night, and seeing this fuckin' speed monitor slide across to floor, use TK and lift an object and just- PFFFFF. Brilliant mental imagery. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think what I like the most is how amazed the doctor seems to be by this whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a very good idea! Notes does fit it far better. I'll do that, thank you.

Thank you for the +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My thoughts as well.

I'd consider changing "Analysis:" to "Notes:", since they aren't really that analytical. I'd be more willing to cut some slack on the tone on research notes than on a professional analysis.
Also, I think kph should be km/h.

Minor nitpicks aside, +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very charming. I'm not so sure about your use of the first person in the test analyses, but i liked the report a lot as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You realize that sight is not the only sense, right? You could touch it, smell it, weigh it, listen to it, take material samples… if one sense is not conclusive, then you use the other ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perfect mix of charming and funny, and I really like the third note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would anyone be interested in writing some more tests to explore these ideas?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder how people would look if he/it recorded them with a camera. Well, another camera.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, this is basically my opinion on this as well. Good enough for an upvote, but really needs some polish. I also agree that the analysis notes should probably be done away with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is now my headcanon that this guy was born as a wee little camcorder and he ate his greens and drank his milk to grow up into a big, strong police speeding-camera.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderful! I'm very encouraged, thank you. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brilliant balance. Funny, but not lolfoundation. I laughed out loud on a crowded bus. Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great! I'm glad I got the balance right for you. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fun to read, completely fucked in the making sense department and just the right amount of whimsy without turning into lolFoundation.

That gets you a solid +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh you are CREEPY!

I am JUST this moment watching a Pewdiepie video of him playing prop hunt. O.O

PS: I'm glad it helped paint an amusing mental image for you. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry to hear you're neutral on this.

Sometimes staff are unprofessional, and I'd say that applies even in the Foundation. Perhaps we're seeing this document in the small window between Littlefield writing it and some higher-ups reprimanding him for being unprofessional and ordering him to tidy it up. It might be a pretty big window, as this is a safe and seemingly harmless SCP, and might not get rigorously and regularly reviewed.

Who knows, maybe Littlefield has bad personal experience with speed cameras, and it's affected his professionalism. They're not the most popular of machines, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of that Prop Hunt game mod, and frankly, I enjoyed imagining a traffic camera hopping onto a chair and lying in a bed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral Vote.

I love the concept, but the unprofessional, petty nature of the "analysis" comments kept me from upvoting. First two are fine, third one is iffy, and the fourth is just unprofessional. I imagine he would just pass the bill to the Foundation and that they have a fund set up for exactly for this sort of thing. He should be more angry over the containment breach than over receiving a bogus ticket.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But you could look at it the other way: it could be a human who has developed (or always had) an unfortunate affliction that he appears to be a speed camera when viewed indirectly.

There's another element in the mix: the fixed penalty notice letter, and the records on the police system to match, suggest some kind of extranormal process is at work: this brand of camera requires detection strips embedded in the road to work, and it also uses film that needs to be physically removed from the camera, neither of which happened.

So if it's capable of manipulating police records, maybe it's capable of other manipulations, up to and including falsifying records of the object's human life (identity records, etc). Who knows. Speculation. :)

Thankfully, the Foundation doesn't need to try and explain it…just keep it locked up. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that's an interesting concept. If a speed camera is magically turned into a walking, talking thing that looks like a person to the naked eye, is it really helpful to consider it as a sapient speed camera? How many speed camera-ish attributes does it need to retain for us to keep on thinking of it as a speed camera that just wants to be human?

I think it's easier for people to understand it as a person that may look like a speed camera than as a sapient speed camera that looks and behaves like a human. But what if that's looking at this thing completely the wrong way round?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But is it, though? By the eye, it looks human. By video/photos, it looks like a speed camera. Which is the truth? This is the question that is making the Doctor's head hurt.

Thank you for the upvote. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Needs to make it clear that it actually is, IRL, a human. Because right now the article just describes the thing's physical appearance under two sets of circumstances.

Upvoting otherwise though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always been OK with research notes as a way of underlining in a sentence or two what the experimenter learned from the last test, and their reasoning for trying something else in the next experiment. Sure it's a deviation from what a real science report would look like, but this is meant to be well-paced fiction, not a seven-page submission to Nature. There's verisimilitude and then there's verbosity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haha! Love it!

If I could upvote a comment, I would. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm now imagining him as a dangerous driver turned into a speed camera by a vengeful, reality-bending cyclist:

Clarkson: "I must destroy the speed cameras!"
The Voice of the Stig (menacing, eerie): "No, Clarkson, you are the speed cameras."

And then Dr. Littlefield was a zombie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you like it enough for an upvote. The tonal issue was something I struggled with. I'll have to address the balance in future: being clinical against having some characterisation for the staff member.

Subject refers to the camera and the man, as they are one and the same thing. Confusion is fine. It's (hopefully) a confusing object/person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you like it. Fridge horror perhaps, seeing what happens to speed cameras a lot of the time.

The identity of the police force is a mystery! Shh! ;)

Well, they do say that you should always know what you put behind those black boxes. I subscribe to that…so the police force (and the town) are correct lengths for some real places.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A big thank you to Photosynthetic, StickFigureNumberOne and bravo104 who gave help and support/encouragement in the forum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but the execution is a little unpolished. I've never really been a huge fan of "analysis notes" from doctors. The notes aren't even analysis, it's peanut gallery commentary. Besides that, there are a few tonal issues, and a few places where I went "really?" in regards to your choice of diction (in retrospect, nothing too major). Small point—I'm pretty sure "subject" is only used for humans in an experiment, which made it really confusing for me when reading the testing log, because you also describe the camera as the "subject."

Eh, it's good enough for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great work - scary in a subtle way.

Incidentally, is the redacted police force meant to be West Yorkshire as an Easter egg, or is it not meant to represent anywhere in particular? I looked up a list as I was curious and it seems to be the only one that fits the redaction…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, I'm glad you like it. I'll happily accept a good, and keep working towards a great. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this just enough to upvote, but it's not great. Maybe it would have worked better if it included an interview. But regardless, this is still good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops, good catch, thank you. I thought I'd caught all typos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of like the idea but there's a few errors in this though like "anomalous nature become known during"
